topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The doorbell rang, a sharp, insistent sound that made me jump. I peered through the peephole, my heart beginning a frantic, uneven rhythm against my ribs. Standing on my welcome mat was a delivery person, holding a medium-sized cardboard box addressed to my apartment number. My stomach clenched, a cold knot tightening in my chest. I knew instantly. This wasn't mine. This was clearly for the folks who had lived here before me. I fumbled with the lock, my hands suddenly clumsy and slick with nervous sweat. As I took the package, the weight of it felt heavy, a physical manifestation of my profound awkwardness. I retreated quickly, trying to melt into the hallway wall, acutely aware of the silent judgment I felt radiating from the anonymous delivery person.

[story 2]

I found the package tucked beside my door after I’d finally managed to get the mail slot open. It was clearly meant for the previous occupant; the handwriting on the label was faded, and the address was undeniably from last month. A wave of heat washed over my face, creeping up to my hairline. I stared at the box, my gaze darting around the hallway as if expecting someone to materialize and point out my transgression. Every muscle in my jaw felt tight. I picked it up, my fingers tracing the rough edges of the packing tape. I needed to get rid of it, to erase this unwelcome intrusion, but my feet felt rooted to the spot. I spent a full minute just standing there, breathing shallowly, the silence of the apartment amplifying the frantic drumming inside my own head.

[story 3]

When the courier handed over the parcel, I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to disappear. The box, addressed to the former resident, felt like a spotlight shining directly on my ineptitude. I mumbled a weak, inarticulate "Oh," and immediately tried to back away, my shoulders hunching inward as if trying to shrink my entire frame. My palms grew damp; I quickly wiped them on my jeans, a futile gesture against the internal tremor. I managed to take the package, clutching it to my chest like a shameful secret. I walked stiffly toward the stairs, my eyes fixed firmly on the floorboards, desperately hoping that the sheer act of moving away would somehow dissipate the uncomfortable feeling that had settled deep in my core.
